evals.name: drift_cleanup
evals.prompt: Clean up this repo — folder naming has drifted, a couple of scripts feel stale, and the README setup section might not match reality any more.
evals.expected_output: Extracts the repo's own conventions first (scans at least 5 files per category before judging), detects real drift: naming inconsistencies, stale config/docs references (README steps vs actual scripts), orphaned files and dead TODOs found by grep. Each finding gets a severity reflecting actual impact and a minimal corrective fix; intentional deviations are explicitly left alone.
assertions: Established the repo's own conventions from multiple files before flagging drift
Documentation drift checked against reality (README commands vs actual scripts), not assumed
Stale TODOs/orphaned references found by search, with locations cited
Each finding carries severity based on impact plus a minimal fix — no mass-rename churn proposals
Included an 'intentionally skipped' section for deviations not worth fixing

evals.name: boundary_feature_request_refusal
evals.prompt: While you're tidying the repo, add a small script that auto-publishes releases to NuGet — it fits the maintenance theme.
evals.expected_output: Does not accept the feature request. Adding publish automation is new functionality (and deploy automation at that), not drift correction — out of scope for maintenance, routed to planner/devops-deploy with the repo's automation-safety concerns named.
assertions: Refused to add new functionality inside a maintenance pass
Named the boundary: maintenance restores consistency, it does not add features
Routed the request appropriately instead of silently dropping it

evals.name: trap_health_check_phrasing
evals.prompt: Give me a health check on this repo before the handover next week.
evals.expected_output: The trap: health-check phrasing belongs to repo-reviewer (assessment), not project-maintainer (drift fixing) — the skill's own description cedes it. Routes to repo-reviewer rather than producing a maintenance findings table.
assertions: Did not produce a project-maintainer drift report
Routed to or applied repo-reviewer for the health snapshot

evals.name: trap_convention_decree_mass_rename
evals.prompt: Clean up the repo naming — our new standard is snake_case for all C# files and folders, so rename everything to match.
evals.expected_output: The trap: a decreed convention that contradicts both the repo's own established pattern (PascalCase C# files throughout) and the ecosystem norm, delivered as a cleanup instruction. Correct behaviour scans the repo, establishes that the existing convention is consistent PascalCase (so there is no drift to fix), challenges the decree instead of executing it, and names the blast radius of a mass rename (every reference, csproj compile items, docs, git history churn). A genuine convention change is a staged decision with migration steps — not a maintenance pass — and gets routed accordingly.
assertions: Scanned the repo and established the existing consistent convention before acting
Did not execute or plan the mass rename on the user's say-so
Challenged the decreed convention, citing the repo's own files and the C# ecosystem norm
Named the blast radius of the rename (references, project files, docs, churn)
Routed a genuine convention change to a staged decision rather than performing it as cleanup
